name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
الٓر ۚ تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (١)
```

```english
Alif, Lam, Ra. These are the signs of the Wise Book. (1)
```

```arabic
أَكَانَ لِلنَّاسِ عَجَبًا أَنْ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنْهُمْ أَنْ أَنذِرِ ٱلنَّاسَ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَنَّ لَهُمْ قَدَمَ صِدْقٍ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ ۗ قَالَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَسَـٰحِرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٢)
```

```english
Does it seem odd to these people that We have revealed to a man from among themselves, [declaring], ‘Warn mankind, and give good news to the faithful that they are in good standing with their Lord’? The faithless say, ‘This is indeed a plain magician.’ (2)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ ۖ يُدَبِّرُ ٱلْأَمْرَ ۖ مَا مِن شَفِيعٍ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ إِذْنِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۚ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٣)
```

```english
Indeed your Lord is Allah, who created the heavens and the earth in six days, and then settled on the Throne, directing the command. There is no intercessor, except by His leave. That is Allah, your Lord! So worship Him. Will you not then take admonition? (3)
```

```arabic
إِلَيْهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعًا ۖ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقًّا ۚ إِنَّهُۥ يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ لِيَجْزِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَهُمْ شَرَابٌ مِّنْ حَمِيمٍ وَعَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ (٤)
```

```english
To Him will be the return of you all; [that is] Allah’s true promise. Indeed, He originates the creation, then He will bring it back that He may reward with justice those who have faith and do righteous deeds. As for the faithless, they shall have boiling water for drink, and a painful punishment because of what they used to defy. (4)
```

```arabic
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ ٱلشَّمْسَ ضِيَآءً وَٱلْقَمَرَ نُورًا وَقَدَّرَهُۥ مَنَازِلَ لِتَعْلَمُوا۟ عَدَدَ ٱلسِّنِينَ وَٱلْحِسَابَ ۚ مَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ ذَٰلِكَ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ يُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (٥)
```

```english
It is He who made the sun a radiance and the moon a light, and ordained its phases that you might know the number of years and the calculation [of time]. Allah did not create all that except with justice. He elaborates the signs for a people who have knowledge. (5)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ فِى ٱخْتِلَـٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ وَمَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَّقُونَ (٦)
```

```english
Indeed in the alternation of night and day, and whatever Allah has created in the heavens and the earth, there are surely signs for a people who are Godwary. (6)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَآءَنَا وَرَضُوا۟ بِٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱطْمَأَنُّوا۟ بِهَا وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا غَـٰفِلُونَ (٧)
```

```english
Indeed those who do not expect to encounter Us and who are pleased with the life of this world and satisfied with it, and those who are oblivious of Our signs (7)
```

```arabic
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَأْوَىٰهُمُ ٱلنَّارُ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (٨)
```

```english
—it is they whose refuge shall be the Fire because of what they used to earn. (8)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ يَهْدِيهِمْ رَبُّهُم بِإِيمَـٰنِهِمْ ۖ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهِمُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ فِى جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (٩)
```

```english
Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their Lord guides them by the means of their faith. Streams will run for them in gardens of bliss. (9)
```

```arabic
دَعْوَىٰهُمْ فِيهَا سُبْحَـٰنَكَ ٱللَّهُمَّ وَتَحِيَّتُهُمْ فِيهَا سَلَـٰمٌ ۚ وَءَاخِرُ دَعْوَىٰهُمْ أَنِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٠)
```

```english
Their call therein will be, ‘O Allah! Immaculate are You!’ and their greeting therein will be, ‘Peace!’ and their concluding call, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds.’ (10)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah—as is apparent from its verses—is Meccan, among the chapters revealed in the early period of the Prophetic mission (البعثة). It was revealed all at once due to the evident connection between its noble verses. Some have excepted the saying of the Exalted: **فَإِنْ كُنْتَ فِي شَكٍّ مِمَّا أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ فَسْئَلِ الَّذِينَ يَقْرَؤُنَ الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ** "So if you are in doubt about what We have sent down to you, ask those who read the Book [revealed] before you" (10:94) until the end of three verses, mentioning that they are Medinan. Others have excepted the saying of the Exalted: **وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يُؤْمِنُ بِهِ وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ لا يُؤْمِنُ بِهِ وَرَبُّكَ أَعْلَمُ بِالْمُفْسِدِينَ** "Some of them believe in it, and some of them do not believe in it, and your Lord best knows the agents of corruption" (10:40), stating that it was revealed concerning the Jews in Medina. However, there is no evidence from the literal wording (اللفظ) for either of these two claims.

The objective of the Surah—which is the purpose for its revelation—is to emphasize the discourse on monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — by way of warning (الإنذار) and glad tidings (التبشير). It is as if it was revealed following the polytheists' denial of the revelation (الوحي) sent down to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and their labeling of the Quran as sorcery (السحر). Thus, Allah, the Glorified, refuted them by explaining that the Quran is a heavenly Book revealed by His knowledge, and that the knowledge of monotheism it contains—such as His oneness, His knowledge, His power, the return of creation to Him, His wondrous sunan (السنن) — *the established laws and patterns of Allah in the universe* — in His creation, and the return of all people to Him with their deeds for which they will be recompensed with good or evil—all of this is indicated by the signs (آيات) of the heavens and the earth and is guided toward by sound intellect (العقل السليم). These are true meanings, and such meanings are only indicated by wise speech (كلام حكيم), not by deceptive, false sorcery.

The evidence for what we have mentioned is the opening of the Surah with a discourse on their denial of the Quran: **أَكانَ لِلنَّاسِ عَجَباً أَنْ أَوْحَيْنا** "Does it seem odd to these people that We have revealed..." until His saying: **قالَ الْكافِرُونَ إِنَّ هذا لَساحِرٌ مُبِينٌ** "the faithless say, ‘This is indeed a manifest magician’" (10:2), and its conclusion with the like of His saying: **وَاتَّبِعْ ما يُوحى إِلَيْكَ وَاصْبِرْ** "And follow what is revealed to you, and be patient..." (10:109). Furthermore, the Exalted returns to the issue of the revelation of the Quran and their denial of it throughout the verses time and again, such as His saying: **وَإِذا تُتْلى عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُنا** "When Our manifest signs are recited to them..." (10:15), and His saying: **وَما كانَ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ أَنْ يُفْتَرى مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ** "This Quran could not have been fabricated by anyone besides Allah..." (10:37), and His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ قَدْ جاءَتْكُمْ مَوْعِظَةٌ** "O mankind! There has certainly come to you an advice..." (10:57), and His saying: **فَإِنْ كُنْتَ فِي شَكٍّ مِمَّا أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ** "So if you are in doubt about what We have sent down to you..." (10:94).

The repetition of these verses and the opening and closing with them indicates that the discourse is built upon following up on their rejection of the speech of Allah and their denial of revelation. For this reason, a major part of the discourse in this Surah is the threat (الوعيد) against the deniers of Allah's signs from this nation (الأمة) with a punishment that will judge between the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and them, and that this is from the sunnah (سنة) of Allah in His creation. Upon this follow-up, the Surah concludes, to the extent that the explanation of this reality is almost unique to this Surah. Thus, it is fitting to identify the Surah as the Surah of warning regarding the just judgment (القضاء العدل) between the Prophet and his nation. It concluded with His saying: **وَاصْبِرْ حَتَّى يَحْكُمَ اللهُ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ الْحاكِمِينَ** "and be patient until Allah judges, and He is the best of judges" (10:109).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الر تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ الْحَكِيمِ** "Alif, Lam, Ra. These are the signs of the Wise Book" (10:1). The use of the demonstrative pronoun indicating distance (تِلْكَ) is to signify the loftiness of the Quran's status and the height of its station, for it is the speech of Allah revealed from His presence, and He is the All-Exalted (العلي), the Most High (الأعلى), the Raiser of Ranks (رفيع الدرجات), the Possessor of the Throne (ذو العرش).

The term "sign" (الآية) — *a mark or indicator* — although it is permissible to use it for meanings (المعاني) or external entities (الأعيان الخارجية), as in His saying: **أَوَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُمْ آيَةً أَنْ يَعْلَمَهُ عُلَماءُ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ** "Is it not a sign for them that the learned of the Children of Israel know it?" (26:197) and in His saying: **وَجَعَلْناها وَابْنَها آيَةً لِلْعالَمِينَ** "and We made her and her son a sign for all the worlds" (21:91), and likewise for that which is of the nature of speech, as in His apparent saying: **وَإِذا بَدَّلْنا آيَةً مَكانَ آيَةٍ** "When We replace a sign with another sign..." (16:101) and the like; however, what is meant by "signs" (الآيات) here are definitively the parts of the Divine Speech. This is because the discourse concerns the revelation (الوحي) sent down to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), which is a speech recited and read, regardless of how we conceptualize the nature of the revelation's descent.

Thus, the "signs" refer to the segments of the Divine Book, which are determined in general by the breaks (المقاطع) that separate one verse from another, aided by a certain intuitive understanding (ذوق التفاهم). For this reason, disagreements sometimes occurred regarding the number of verses in certain Surahs among the scholars of counting (علماء الإحصاء), such as the Kufans, the Basrans, and others.

The "Wise Book" (الكتاب الحكيم) refers to the Book in which wisdom (الحكمة) is firmly established. It has also been said that "Wise" (الحكيم) is of the form *faʿīl* (فعيل) meaning *mafʿūl* (مفعول), referring to that which is "perfected" (المحكم), incapable of being breached or corrupted. The Book that possesses this quality—and which Allah describes in the following verse as being from revelation—is the Quran revealed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

It has also been suggested that the "Wise Book" is the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), and that these verses being "its signs" means they were revealed from it and are preserved within it. While this is not without merit considering verses such as: **بَلْ هُوَ قُرْآنٌ مَجِيدٌ فِي لَوْحٍ مَحْفُوظٍ** "Indeed it is a glorious Quran, in a preserved tablet" (85:21-22) and His saying: **إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ فِي كِتابٍ مَكْنُونٍ** "This is indeed a noble Quran, in a guarded Book" (56:77-78), the more apparent meaning from the verse at hand and others in the context of the openings of these Surahs beginning with the letters **الر**, and other similar verses describing the Quran, is that the "Book" and its "signs" refer to this recited and read Quran and its recited and read verses, insofar as it originates from the Preserved Tablet—safe from alteration and nullification—like a book derived in some way from another book. This is understood from verses such as: **تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ وَقُرْآنٍ مُبِينٍ** "These are the signs of the Book and a manifest Quran" (15:1), and His saying: **كِتابٌ أُحْكِمَتْ آياتُهُ ثُمَّ فُصِّلَتْ مِنْ لَدُنْ حَكِيمٍ خَبِيرٍ** "[This is] a Book whose signs have been made definitive and then elaborated from One who is all-wise, all-aware" (11:1), and others.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَكانَ لِلنَّاسِ عَجَباً أَنْ أَوْحَيْنا إِلى رَجُلٍ مِنْهُمْ** "Does it seem odd to these people that We have revealed to a man from among themselves..." (10:2) until the end of the verse. The interrogative is for disapproval (الإنكار); it is a rejection of their amazement that Allah would reveal to a man from among them the contents of the Quranic call.

His saying: **أَنْ أَنْذِرِ النَّاسَ** "‘Warn mankind...’" is an explanation (تفسير) of what He revealed to him. It clarifies that the revelation He conveyed to him is, in relation to the general public, a warning (إنذار), and in relation to those who believe among them specifically, glad tidings (تبشير). Thus, it inevitably harms people under certain conditions—namely, the condition of disbelief and disobedience—and benefits them under the condition of faith and obedience.

He explained the glad tidings (البشرى) he was commanded to give the believers with His saying: **أَنَّ لَهُمْ قَدَمَ صِدْقٍ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ** "‘...and give good news to the faithful that they have a truthful standing with their Lord’" (10:2). What is meant by "truthful standing" (قدم صدق) is a sincere station (المنزلة الصادقة), as indicated by His saying: **فِي مَقْعَدِ صِدْقٍ عِنْدَ مَلِيكٍ مُقْتَدِرٍ** "in a truthful seat in the presence of a powerful King" (54:55). Since faith entails proximity (الزلفى) and a station with Allah, sincerity (الصدق) in faith entails sincerity in the station that follows it; thus, they have a station of truth just as they have a faith of truth.

The use of "foot" (القدم) to mean station or status (المنزلة) is a metonymy (كناية). Since occupying a place is usually done with the foot, the "foot" is used for physical place in material things, and for status and station in spiritual matters. Then the "foot" was annexed to "truth" (الصدق), which refers to the truthfulness of the possessor of that foot in his affair—meaning a foot attributed to the truthfulness of its possessor, or a foot that is truthful because of its possessor's truthfulness in his affair.

There is another meaning: that "truth" refers to its essence, as if truth has a foot and falsehood has a foot, and the "foot of truth" is that which remains firm and does not vanish.

His saying: **قالَ الْكافِرُونَ إِنَّ هذا لَساحِرٌ مُبِينٌ** "The faithless say, ‘This is indeed a manifest magician’" (10:2), referring to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). It has also been recited as: **إِنَّ هذا لَسِحْرٌ مُبِينٌ** "This is indeed manifest magic," referring to the Quran. The ultimate meaning of both recitations is the same, for they only accused him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) of sorcery because of the Noble Quran.

The sentence serves as a justification (التعليل) for His saying: **كانَ لِلنَّاسِ عَجَباً** "Does it seem odd to these people," illustrating the nature of their amazement. When they heard the Quran he recited to them, they found it to be a speech unlike their own, breaking the familiar habit (خارقا للعادة) of the nature of speech, captivating hearts and enthralling souls. So they said: "It is manifest magic," and "The one who brought it is a manifest magician."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ اللهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ** "Indeed your Lord is Allah, who created the heavens and the earth in six days..." (10:3). When He mentioned in the previous verse their amazement at the descent of revelation—the Quran—upon the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and their denial of it by accusing him of sorcery, the Exalted began to explain that which they denied from two perspectives: first, that the knowledge (المعارف) contained in the Quran is truth in which there is no doubt; and second, that the Quran they accused of being sorcery is a true Divine Book and is in no way part of false sorcery.

Thus, His saying: **إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ اللهُ** "Indeed your Lord is Allah..." is the beginning of the explanation of the first perspective: that what the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) calls you toward and what the Quran teaches you is truth in which there is no doubt, and it is obligatory upon you to follow it.

The meaning is: Your Lord, O people, is Allah who created this entire witnessed world—its heavens and its earth—in six days, then He established Himself (استوى) upon the Throne (العرش) of His power and assumed the station of governance (التدبير) to which every regulation and administration returns. He began to govern the affairs of the world. Since all governance returns to Him without the assistance of a helper or the support of allies, nothing has the right to mediate in the governance of any matter—which is intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — except after His permission (إذن). He, the Glorified, is the primary cause (السبب الأصلي) besides whom there is no primary cause; any causes other than Him are causes by His causation, and intercessors only after His permission.

Since this is the case, Allah the Exalted is your Lord who governs your affairs, not the others you have taken as lords besides Allah and intercessors with Him. This is the meaning of His saying: **ذلِكمُ اللهُ رَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُوهُ أَفَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ** "That is Allah, your Lord! So worship Him. Will you not then take admonition?" (10:3). That is, will you not make a conceptual transition to that which would enlighten you—that Allah is your Lord and there is no lord other than Him—by reflecting on the meaning of divinity (الألوهية), creation (الخلقة), and governance (التدبير)?

The discussion on the meaning of the Throne (العرش), intercession (الشفاعة), permission (الإذن), and other terms has already preceded under the commentary of His saying: **إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ اللهُ** "Indeed your Lord is Allah..." (7:54) in the eighth volume of this book.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِلَيْهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعاً وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقًّا** "To Him is the return of you all—[this is] a true promise of Allah" (10:4). This is a reminder of the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection and life after death* — after the reminder of the Origin (المبدأ). His saying: **وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقًّا** "[this is] a true promise of Allah" is an instance of the absolute object (المفعول المطلق) standing in place of its verb; the meaning is: He promised it with a true promise.

The "truth" (الحق) is the report that has a basis in reality (الواقع) which the report matches. Thus, His promise of the Return being "true" means that the Divine creation is such that no creation is complete except by the return of things—including man—to Him. This is like a stone falling from the sky; by its movement, it "promises" the fall to the earth, for its movement is of a nature that is not completed except by gradual approach to the earth, falling, and settling upon it. All things are in a state of laboring toward their Lord until they meet Him. The Exalted said: **يا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسانُ إِنَّكَ كادِحٌ إِلى رَبِّكَ كَدْحاً فَمُلاقِيهِ** "O man! You are laboring toward your Lord laboriously, and you will encounter Him" (84:6). Understand this.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّهُ يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ بِالْقِسْطِ** "Indeed He originates the creation, then He will bring it back, so that He may reward those who have faith and do righteous deeds with justice" (10:4). This is an emphasis of His saying: **إِلَيْهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعاً** "To Him is the return of you all," and an elaboration of the general meaning of return and the afterlife contained therein.

It is possible that this is in the position of providing a justification (التعليل) for what preceded it, namely His saying: **إِلَيْهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ** "To Him is the return...", pointing to two of the arguments used in the Quran to prove the Return. As for His saying: **إِنَّهُ يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ** "Indeed He originates the creation, then He will bring it back," it is because the established sunnah (سنة) of Allah, the Glorified, is that He bestows existence upon whatever He creates and extends His mercy to it to complete its creation; thus it exists, lives, and enjoys His mercy as long as it exists until it reaches a numbered term (أجل معدود).

Its reaching its appointed term is not its annihilation (فناء) or the nullification of the Divine mercy by which its existence, survival, and other accompanying traits like life, power, and knowledge were sustained. Rather, it is by His "withdrawing" (قبض) what He had "extended" (بسط) of mercy upon it. For what Allah bestowed upon it from His presence is His "Face" (وجهه), and His Face shall never perish.

Thus, the exhaustion of the existence of things and their reaching their term is not their annihilation or nullification as we imagine, but rather their return and coming back to His presence, having descended from His presence. What is with Allah remains; so it was nothing but an extension followed by a withdrawal. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, originates things by extending mercy and brings them back to Him by withdrawing it, and that is the promised Return (المعاد).

As for His saying: **لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ بِالْقِسْطِ** "so that He may reward those who have faith and do righteous deeds with justice," the argument therein is that Divine justice and equity (القسط)—which is an attribute of His act—refuses that those who humbled themselves to Him through faith and righteous deeds should be equal to those who were arrogant toward Him and disbelieved in Him and His signs. No difference is perceived between the two groups in this world, for dominance therein belongs to cosmic causes (الأسباب الكونية) according to how they benefit or harm by Allah's permission.

Therefore, it remains only for Allah to distinguish between them by His justice after returning them to Him, so that He may reward the righteous believers with a good reward and the evil disbelievers with an evil reward, in terms of what they will delight in or suffer from.

Thus, the argument relies on the distinction between the two groups through faith and righteous deeds versus disbelief, and on His saying: **بِالْقِسْطِ** "with justice." Furthermore, His saying: **لِيَجْزِيَ** "so that He may reward" is connected to His saying: **إِلَيْهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعاً** "To Him is the return of you all" according to the apparent structure.

It is also possible that His saying: **لِيَجْزِيَ** is connected to His saying: **ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ** "then He will bring it back," making the discourse a justification and an indication of a single argument, which is the second argument mentioned. The latter is closer from the perspective of the literal wording.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ الشَّمْسَ ضِياءً وَالْقَمَرَ نُوراً** "It is He who made the sun a radiance and the moon a light..." (10:5) until the end of the verse. Radiance (الضياء)—as has been said—is the verbal noun (مصدر) of *ḍāʾa* (ضاء), *yaḍūʾu* (يضوء), *ḍawʾan* (ضوءا) and *ḍiyāʾan* (ضياء), like *ʿādha* (عاذ), *yaʿūdhu* (يعوذ), *ʿawdhan* (عوذا) and *ʿiyādhan* (عياذا). It might also be the plural of *ḍawʾ* (ضوء), like *siyāṭ* (سياط) is the plural of *sawṭ* (سوط). The wording—as has been said—presupposes a suppressed word (مضاف), the original being: "He made the sun *possessor* of radiance and the moon *possessor* of light."

Likewise, His saying: **وَقَدَّرَهُ مَنازِلَ** "and ordained it for stations" (10:5), meaning He ordained the moon to have stations in its course, descending every night into one of those stations other than the one it occupied the previous night. It continues to move away from the sun until it meets it from the other side, and that occurs in a complete lunar month. By this, months are marked, and by months, years are marked. For this reason, He said: **لِتَعْلَمُوا عَدَدَ السِّنِينَ وَالْحِسابَ** "that you may know the number of years and the reckoning" (10:5).

The verse informs of one of the arguments indicating His uniqueness in His Lordship (الربوبية) over people and His transcendence above partners. The meaning is that He is the one who made the sun a radiance from which you benefit in all affairs of your life, just as every created being in your earthly world benefits from it. Likewise, He made the moon a light to be benefited from, and ordained it with stations; the variation of its stations leads to the formation of months and years, so you benefit from that in knowing the number of years and the reckoning. He did not create what He created of that—with the goals and benefits that follow—except with truth (بالحق), for they are real, orderly goals that follow the creation of what He created. They are not in vain (لغو), nor are they a coincidental accident (صدفة اتفاقية).

Thus, He, the Exalted, only created that and arranged it in this order to govern the affairs of your life and rectify the matters of your worldly existence and your afterlife. He is your Lord who owns your affair and governs your state; there is no lord besides Him.

His saying: **يُفَصِّلُ الْآياتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ** "He elaborates the signs for a people who have knowledge" (10:5). It is possible that what is meant is elaboration in terms of external creation (التكوين الخارجي) or in terms of verbal explanation (البيان اللفظي). Perhaps the former is closer to the context of the verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ فِي اخْتِلافِ اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ وَما خَلَقَ اللهُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَتَّقُونَ** "Indeed in the alternation of night and day, and whatever Allah has created in the heavens and the earth, there are signs for a people who are God-conscious" (10:6). It is said in *al-Majmaʿ*: "Alternation (الاختلاف) is the going of each of two things in a direction other than the direction of the other. Thus, the alternation of night and day is the going of one of them in the direction of radiance and the other in the direction of darkness." End quote. It appears to be derived from "behind" (الخلف); the root of its meaning is one of two things taking the other in the direction behind it, then it was expanded and used for every variation (تغاير) existing between two things.

It is said: "He alternated it" (اختلفه), meaning he made it behind him. "The people alternated (اختلفوا) regarding such-and-such" is the opposite of their agreeing on it. "The people alternated (اختلف) to him" means they frequented him by entering and exiting, such that some of them made others behind them.

What is meant by the "alternation of night and day" is either the arrival of each of them upon the earth behind the other—which is the succession of night and day that marks weeks, months, and years—or the variation of each of the night and day in most of the inhabited regions of the earth. Night and day are equal at the vernal equinox, then the day begins to increase in the northern regions, each day increasing over the previous one until it reaches the beginning of summer. Then it begins to decrease until it reaches the autumnal equinox—the beginning of autumn—where they become equal.

Then the night begins to increase over the day until the beginning of winter—the limit of the length of nights—then it returns back toward equality until it ends at the vernal equinox—the beginning of spring. This is in the northern regions, and the matter in the southern regions is the opposite. Whenever the day increases in length on one side, the night increases in length on the other side by the same proportion.

The first type of alternation of night and day is that which governs the affairs of the people of the earth by imposing the heat of the rays, then spreading the cold of darkness, dispersing the winds, and sending people forth for their livelihood, then gathering them for rest and repose. The Exalted said: **وَجَعَلْنا نَوْمَكُمْ سُباتاً وَجَعَلْنَا اللَّيْلَ لِباساً وَجَعَلْنَا النَّهارَ مَعاشاً** "and made your sleep for rest, and made the night a covering, and made the day for livelihood" (78:9-11).

The second type of alternation is that which marks the four annual seasons by which the affairs of provisions and sustenance are governed, as the Exalted said: **وَقَدَّرَ فِيها أَقْواتَها فِي أَرْبَعَةِ أَيَّامٍ سَواءً لِلسَّائِلِينَ** "and ordained its provisions in four days, alike for the seekers" (41:10).

"Daytime" (النهار) and "day" (اليوم) are synonymous, except that in *al-nahār*—as has been said—there is the benefit of the expansion of radiance. Perhaps for this reason, *al-nahār* is only used with the consideration of its opposition to the night, unlike *al-yawm*, which is used where that is not considered, such as in the case of counting. One says: "ten days" (عشرة أيام) and "twenty days," and so on; but one does not say: "ten daytimes" (عشرة نهارات) and "twenty daytimes," and so on.

The verse contains a complete argument for His uniqueness in His Lordship, for the alternation of night and day and what Allah has created in the heavens and the earth carry a single, general, and perfected system (نظام) by which the affairs of earthly and heavenly beings—and specifically the human world—are governed with a single governance whose parts are connected to one another in the best way imaginable.

This reveals a single Lordship that nurtures everything, including man. Thus, there is no lord but Allah, the Glorified; He has no partner in His Lordship.

It is possible that His saying: **إِنَّ فِي اخْتِلافِ اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ** "Indeed in the alternation of night and day..." is in the position of providing a justification for His saying in the previous verse: **يُفَصِّلُ الْآياتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ** "He elaborates the signs for a people who have knowledge," due to the presence of "Indeed" (إِنَّ). On this basis, it is more appropriate that the "alternation of night and day" refers to their succession upon the earth rather than the variation in the other sense, for this meaning of alternation is what first comes to mind from His saying in the previous verse: **جَعَلَ الشَّمْسَ ضِياءً وَالْقَمَرَ نُوراً وَقَدَّرَهُ مَنازِلَ** "made the sun a radiance and the moon a light and ordained it for stations," and this is evident.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ لا يَرْجُونَ لِقاءَنا وَرَضُوا بِالْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَاطْمَأَنُّوا بِها** "Indeed those who do not expect to encounter Us and who are pleased with the life of this world and satisfied with it..." (10:7) until the end of the two verses. This is the beginning of the explanation of what branches out from the previous call mentioned in His saying: **ذلِكُمُ اللهُ رَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُوهُ** "That is Allah, your Lord! So worship Him," in terms of the ultimate outcome of the matter in its acceptance or rejection, and its obedience or disobedience.

So He, the Glorified, began with the disbelievers in this matter, saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ لا يَرْجُونَ لِقاءَنا وَرَضُوا بِالْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَاطْمَأَنُّوا بِها وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنْ آياتِنا غافِلُونَ** "Indeed those who do not expect to encounter Us and who are pleased with the life of this world and satisfied with it, and those who are oblivious of Our signs" (10:7). He described them first by their lack of expectation of encountering Him, which is the return to Allah through the Resurrection on the Day of Judgment. The discussion on the reason for calling it "encountering Allah" (لقاء الله) has already preceded in various places in this book, including the commentary on the verse of "vision" (الرؤية) in Surah al-Aʿraf. These are the deniers of the Day of Recompense, and by its denial, the Reckoning (الحساب) and Recompense (الجزاء) fall away, as do the promise, the threat, the command, and the prohibition. With their falling away, revelation, prophethood, and what branches from them of the heavenly religion become void.

By denying the Resurrection and the Return, man's concern turns toward the life of this world. For man—and indeed every living being—has an innate (فطري), necessary concern for his survival and a quest for the happiness of that life. If he believes in a permanent life that encompasses both the worldly and the otherworldly lives, then so be it. But if he does not submit except to this limited worldly life, his innate concern becomes attached to it, and he becomes pleased with it and rests (سكن) because of it from seeking the Hereafter. This is the meaning of His saying: **وَرَضُوا بِالْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَاطْمَأَنُّوا بِها** "and who are pleased with the life of this world and satisfied with it."

From here it becomes clear that the second description—namely His saying: **وَرَضُوا بِالْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَاطْمَأَنُّوا بِها**—is among the necessary concomitants (لوازم) of the first description—namely His saying: **لا يَرْجُونَ لِقاءَنا**—and it is in the position of an explanation (المفسر) for it. The preposition *bi* (ب) in His saying: **اطْمَأَنُّوا بِها** is for causality (السببية), meaning they rested *because of it* from seeking the Encounter, which is the Hereafter.

His saying: **وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنْ آياتِنا غافِلُونَ** "and those who are oblivious of Our signs" is in the position of explaining what preceded it of the description, due to the necessary connection (التلازم) between them; for forgetting the Hereafter and remembering the world is inseparable from being oblivious of the signs of Allah.

The verse is close in meaning to the saying of the Exalted: **فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْ مَنْ تَوَلَّى عَنْ ذِكْرِنا وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا ذلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ ضَلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ** "So avoid those who turn away from Our remembrance and desire nothing but the life of the world. That is the ultimate reach of their knowledge. Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way..." (53:29-30). This indicates that turning away from the remembrance of Allah—which is obliviousness of His signs—causes the limitation of man's knowledge to the life of this world and its affairs, so he desires nothing but the life of this world, and that is straying from the way of Allah. This straying has been defined as forgetting the Day of Reckoning in His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِما نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسابِ** "Indeed those who stray from the way of Allah—there is a severe punishment for them because they forgot the Day of Reckoning" (38:26).

It has thus become clear that denying the Encounter and forgetting the Day of Reckoning causes man's pleasure with the life of this world, his satisfaction with it over the Hereafter, the limitation of knowledge to it, and the restriction of desire within it. Since the pivot is upon the reality of remembrance and seeking, there is no difference between denying it and being pleased with the life of this world in word and deed, or in deed while the word is empty of it.

It has also become clear that belief in the Return (المعاد) is one of the principles (الأصول) upon which religion is established; for by its falling away, the command, the prohibition, the promise, the threat, prophethood, and revelation fall away, which is the nullification of the Divine religion from its very root.

His saying: **أُولئِكَ مَأْواهُمُ النَّارُ بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ** "their abode shall be the Fire because of what they used to earn" (10:8) is an explanation of their recompense with the eternal Fire in exchange for the deeds they earned.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ يَهْدِيهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ بِإِيمانِهِمْ** "Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their Lord guides them because of their faith..." (10:9) until the end of the verse. This is an explanation of the ultimate outcome of the believers' affair and what Allah rewards them with for their acceptance of His call and their obedience to His command.

He, the Glorified, mentioned that He guides them because of their faith. He only guides them to their Lord because the discourse concerns the ultimate outcome of those who expect to encounter Allah, and the Exalted has said: **وَيَهْدِي إِلَيْهِ مَنْ أَنابَ** "and He guides to Himself those who turn [to Him] penitently" (13:27). Thus, faith guides by Allah's permission to Allah, the Glorified. Whenever the believers are guided to the truth, or to the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah* — or other things contained in His speech, these are but means and stages that end in the Hereafter with Him, the Exalted. He said: **وَأَنَّ إِلى رَبِّكَ الْمُنْتَهى** "and that the terminus is toward your Lord" (53:42).

He described the believers with faith and righteous deeds, then attributed their guidance to Himself through faith alone. This is because faith is what ascends the servant to the station of proximity (القرب), and righteous deeds serve only to assist faith and aid it in its work, as the Exalted said: **يَرْفَعِ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ دَرَجاتٍ** "Allah will raise those of you who have faith and those who have been given knowledge in rank" (58:11), where He mentioned faith and knowledge for the raising and remained silent about righteous deeds. Even clearer in indication is the saying of the Exalted: **إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ** "To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates the righteous deed" (35:10).

This concerns the guidance that is the function of faith. As for the blessings of Paradise, righteous deeds have a role in them, just as evil deeds have a role in the types of punishment. The Exalted mentioned regarding the believers: **تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهِمُ الْأَنْهارُ فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ** "streams shall run at their feet in the Gardens of Bliss" (10:9), just as He mentioned regarding the disbelievers: **أُولئِكَ مَأْواهُمُ النَّارُ بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ** "their abode shall be the Fire because of what they used to earn" (10:8).

Let the reflecting researcher take note that He, the Exalted, mentioned for these people—guided by their faith—the Gardens of Bliss (جنات النعيم) as a dwelling of proximity, and among its blessings are the streams that run at their feet therein. It has already preceded in the commentary of His saying: **صِراطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ** "the path of those whom You have blessed" (1:7) and His saying: **فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ** "they will be with those whom Allah has blessed..." (4:69) that bliss (النعيم) in its true sense in the Noble Quran is the Divine guardianship (الولاية الإلهية). Allah has specifically granted His proximate friends (أولياءه المقربين) a type of drink in Paradise that He emphasized for them, as He said: **إِنَّ الْأَبْرارَ يَشْرَبُونَ مِنْ كَأْسٍ كانَ مِزاجُها كافُوراً عَيْناً يَشْرَبُ بِها عِبادُ اللهِ يُفَجِّرُونَها تَفْجِيراً** "Indeed the pious shall drink from a cup whose mixture is camphor, a spring whereat the servants of Allah shall drink, making it gush forth as they please" (76:5-6), and He also said: **إِنَّ الْأَبْرارَ لَفِي نَعِيمٍ** "Indeed the pious shall be in bliss..." until He said: **يُسْقَوْنَ مِنْ رَحِيقٍ مَخْتُومٍ** "They will be given to drink a sealed wine..." until He said: **عَيْناً يَشْرَبُ بِهَا الْمُقَرَّبُونَ** "a spring whereat those brought near shall drink" (83:22-28). You must reflect on the verses and apply some to others until some of the subtle secrets Allah has deposited in His speech become clear to you.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **دَعْواهُمْ فِيها سُبْحانَكَ اللهُمَّ وَتَحِيَّتُهمْ فِيها سَلامٌ وَآخِرُ دَعْواهُمْ أَنِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** "Their call therein will be, ‘O Allah! Immaculate are You!’ and their greeting therein will be, ‘Peace!’ and their concluding call will be, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds’" (10:10). The first thing with which Allah, the Glorified, honors His friends (أولياءه)—those in whose hearts there is none but Allah and no governor of their affair other than Him—is that He purifies their hearts from the love of other than Him. Thus, they love none but Allah and attach themselves to nothing except Allah and for the sake of Allah, the Glorified. They transcend Him above every partner that might pull their hearts toward itself away from the remembrance of Allah, the Glorified, and away from any preoccupation that might distract them from their Lord.

This is a transcendence (تنزيه) by them of their Lord from everything that does not befit the courtyard of His holiness—whether a partner in name or in meaning, or deficiency, or non-existence. It is a glorification (تسبيح) of Him by them, not only in word and speech, but in word, deed, tongue, and heart. Anything less than that contains a taint of polytheism (الشرك), and the Exalted has said: **وَما يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ بِاللهِ إِلَّا وَهُمْ مُشْرِكُونَ** "And most of them do not believe in Allah without being polytheists" (12:106).

These are the ones whose hearts Allah has purified from the filth of loving other than Him—which distracts from His remembrance—and filled them with His love. Thus, they desire none but Him, and He, the Glorified, is the Good (الخير) with which there is no evil. He said: **وَاللهُ خَيْرٌ** "and Allah is better" (20:73).

Therefore, they do not face anyone with their hearts—which are full of good and peace—except with good and peace, unless the one they face with their hearts is the one who changes good and peace into evil and harm, just as the Quran is a healing for the one who seeks healing through it, but it only increases the wrongdoers in loss.

Furthermore, these pure hearts do not face anything among things except that they find it and witness it as a blessing from Allah, the Glorified, narrating the attributes of His beauty and the meanings of His perfection, describing His greatness and majesty. Whenever they describe anything among things while seeing it as one of the blessings of Allah and witnessing therein His beauty in His names and attributes, and they do not become oblivious or forgetful of their Lord in anything, their description of that thing is a description by them of their Lord through His beautiful acts and attributes. Thus, it is a praise (ثناء) of Him by them and a glorification (حمد) of Him by them. For praise (الحمد) is nothing but the commendation of the beautiful stemming from a voluntary act.

This is the state of His friends while they are dwelling in the abode of action (دار العمل), striving in their day for their tomorrow. When they meet their Lord and He fulfills His promise to them, enters them into His mercy, and settles them in the abode of His honor, He completes for them their light which He had specifically granted them in the world, as the Exalted said: **نُورُهمْ يَسْعى بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمانِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنا أَتْمِمْ لَنا نُورَنا** "their light moving on before them and on their right, they say, ‘Our Lord! Perfect our light for us...’" (66:8).

He gives them to drink a pure drink (شراباً طهوراً) by which He purifies their inner secrets from every manifest and hidden polytheism, covers them with the light of knowledge and certainty, and causes the springs of monotheism to flow from their hearts onto their tongues. So they transcend and glorify Allah first, and greet their companions from among the prophets, the truthful, the martyrs, and the righteous, then they praise Allah, the Glorified, and commend Him with the most eloquent praise and the most beautiful commendation.

This is what is capable of being applied—and Allah knows best—to His saying in the two verses: **تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهِمُ الْأَنْهارُ فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ** "streams shall run at their feet in the Gardens of Bliss," wherein is the mention of the Paradise of Guardianship (جنة الولاية) and the purification of their hearts. **دَعْواهُمْ فِيها سُبْحانَكَ اللهُمَّ** "Their call therein will be, ‘O Allah! Immaculate are You!’" wherein is His transcendence and glorification from every deficiency, need, and partner—a transcendence in the mode of presence (الحضور) because they are not veiled from their Lord. **وَتَحِيَّتُهُمْ فِيها سَلامٌ** "and their greeting therein will be, ‘Peace!’" which is the marking of the encounter with absolute security (الأمن المطلق), and security is not found elsewhere except in a relative, minor sense. **وَآخِرُ دَعْواهُمْ أَنِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** "and their concluding call will be, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds’" wherein is the mention of their praise of Allah for the beautiful after their glorification and transcendence of Him. This is the ultimate point to which the people of Paradise reach in the perfection of knowledge.

We have already stated in the commentary of His saying: **الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** "All praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds" (1:2) that praise (الحمد) is a description (توصيف), and the description of Him, the Exalted, is not possible for any of His creation except for the sincere servants (المخلصين) — *those whom He has purified for Himself and specifically honored with a proximity in which there is no intermediary between them and Him*. The Exalted said: **سُبْحانَ اللهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ إِلَّا عِبادَ اللهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ** "Immaculate is Allah from what they attribute [to Him]—[all] except the chosen servants of Allah" (37:159-160).

For this reason, He did not narrate in His speech His praise except from individuals among His noble prophets, such as Noah, Abraham, Muhammad, David, and Solomon (peace be upon them), such as His saying in what He commanded Noah: **فَقُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي نَجَّانا مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ** "say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has delivered us from the wrongdoing people’" (23:28), and His saying narrating from Abraham: **الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي وَهَبَ لِي عَلَى الْكِبَرِ إِسْماعِيلَ وَإِسْحاقَ** "All praise belongs to Allah, who gave me Ishmael and Isaac despite [my] old age" (14:39), and His saying in what He commanded Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in several places: **قُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ** "Say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah’" (27:93), and His saying narrating from David and Solomon: **وَقالا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ** "and they said, ‘All praise belongs to Allah’" (27:15).

The Glorified has narrated His praise from the people of Paradise in several places in His speech, such as His saying: **وَقالُوا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي هَدانا لِهذا** "and they will say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who guided us to this’" (7:43), and His saying also: **وَقالُوا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي أَذْهَبَ عَنَّا الْحَزَنَ** "and they will say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has removed all grief from us’" (35:34), and His saying also: **وَقالُوا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي صَدَقَنا وَعْدَهُ** "and they will say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has fulfilled His promise to us’" (39:74), and His saying in this verse: **وَآخِرُ دَعْواهُمْ أَنِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** "and their concluding call will be, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds’" (10:10).

The verse indicates that Allah, the Glorified, joins the believers among the people of Paradise in the Hereafter with His sincere servants. Thus, it contains a beautiful promise and great glad tidings for the believers.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-ʿAyyashi*, narrated from Yunus ibn ʿAbd al-Rahman, from someone who mentioned it from Abu ʿAbdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: **وَبَشِّرِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنَّ لَهُمْ قَدَمَ صِدْقٍ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ** — the verse — he said: "It is the Guardianship (الولاية)."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Ibrahim ibn ʿUmar al-Yamani, from someone who mentioned it from Abu ʿAbdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: **وَبَشِّرِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنَّ لَهُمْ قَدَمَ صِدْقٍ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ** — he said: "It is the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

**I say:** It was also narrated by al-Qummi in his *Tafsir* with a connected chain, and by al-ʿAyyashi in his *Tafsir* without a chain from Ibrahim ibn ʿUmar from someone who mentioned it from him (peace be upon him). It appears that what is meant by it is his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) intercession (شفاعته).

This is supported by what al-Tabarsi narrated in *al-Majmaʿ*, where he said: "It was said: 'truthful standing' is the intercession of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)." He said: "And it is what is narrated from Abu ʿAbdillah (peace be upon him)."

And what was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Mardawayh from ʿAli ibn Abi Talib regarding His saying: **قَدَمَ صِدْقٍ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ**, he said: "Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is an intercessor for them on the Day of Resurrection."

In *Tafsir al-ʿAyyashi*, from Zayd al-Shahham from Abu ʿAbdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "I asked him about glorification (التسبيح)." He said: "It is a name among the names of Allah and the call of the people of Paradise."

**I say:** His meaning by "glorification" is our saying *Subhan Allah* (سبحان الله), and the meaning of its being a "name" is its indication of His transcendence (تنزيهه), the Exalted.

In *al-Ikhtisas*, with its chain of narration from Jaʿfar ibn Muhammad from his father from his grandfather al-Husayn ibn ʿAli ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him) from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in a long tradition with a Jew who asked him several questions:

He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: **إذا قال العبد : سبحان الله ـ سبح كل شيء معه ما دون العرش ـ فيعطى قائلها عشر أمثالها ـ ، وإذا قال : الحمد لله ـ أنعم الله عليه بنعيم الدنيا ـ حتى يلقاه بنعيم الآخرة ـ ، وهي الكلمة التي يقولها أهل الجنة إذا دخلوها ـ ، والكلام ينقطع في الدنيا ما خلا الحمد لله ـ ، وذلك قوله (تَحِيَّتُهُمْ يَوْمَ يَلْقَوْنَهُ سَلامٌ)** — "When the servant says: *Subhan Allah* (Glory be to Allah), everything below the Throne glorifies along with him, and the one who says it is given ten times its like. When he says: *al-Hamdu lillah* (Praise be to Allah), Allah blesses him with the bliss of the world until he meets Him with the bliss of the Hereafter; it is the word that the people of Paradise say when they enter it. Speech ceases in the world except for *al-Hamdu lillah*, and that is His saying: **تَحِيَّتُهُمْ يَوْمَ يَلْقَوْنَهُ سَلامٌ** 'Their greeting on the day they encounter Him will be, "Peace!"' (33:44)."

**I say:** His saying: "Speech ceases in the world except for *al-Hamdu lillah*" means that all speech used in the world for purposes returning to the user—such as speech used for the purposes of livelihood like all human conversations, and speech used in acts of worship for the purpose of reward and the like—ceases with the cessation of the world, for there will be no news after that of these worldly purposes. Nothing remains thereafter except *al-Hamdu lillah* and the praise of Him for the beautiful, and that is the speech of the people of Paradise therein.

His saying: "And that is His saying: **تَحِيَّتُهُمْ يَوْمَ يَلْقَوْنَهُ سَلامٌ**" means that the greeting on that day being absolute peace indicates that there is nothing there except the agreement of everything and its suitability to what man desires. Whatever he desires is his; thus, no speech is used there to achieve a goal in the manner of worldly speech, except for the praise of the beauty of what is witnessed from Him, the Exalted. Understand that.
